description: Figure 1 presents X-ray diffraction patterns of Nb-containing medium-Mn low-density steels, specifically Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C with 0.02Nb (panel a) and 0.04Nb (panel b). Each panel displays four stacked curves for the as-cast condition and aging at 723 K, 773 K, and 823 K for 3 h. The peaks are indexed to austenite γ [(111), (200), (220), (311), (222)] and delta-ferrite δ [(110), (200), (211)]. As stated in the caption, the diffraction peak intensities of both austenite and ferrite show almost no significant change after aging at different temperatures for either Nb content. The stable dual-phase constitution across all curves indicates that the bulk ferrite-austenite phase balance is preserved regardless of Nb level or aging temperature. This supports the core conclusion that Nb addition governs microstructural evolution and mechanical properties primarily through grain refinement and precipitation behavior rather than modifying the overall phase assembly.

Figure 2 presents backscattered-electron SEM micrographs showing the dual-phase microstructure of Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C low-density steels containing 0.02Nb (a–d) and 0.04Nb (e–h), in the as-cast state and after aging at 723, 773, and 823 K for 3 h. In all micrographs, irregular δ-ferrite particles (yellow arrows, labeled δ) are randomly dispersed within the continuous γ-austenite matrix (labeled γ), while submicron white NbC precipitates are indicated by purple arrows; the scale bar is 20 μm. Visual comparison reveals that the 0.04Nb steel exhibits a markedly finer austenite grain size than the 0.02Nb steel. Caption-reported ImageJ measurements indicate ferrite contents of approximately 16.2, 16.4, 17.1, and 18.3 vol% for the 0.02Nb series, and 16.3, 17.2, 18.4, and 18.1 vol% for the 0.04Nb series, suggesting the ferrite fraction is only weakly affected by Nb content and aging temperature. The micrographs collectively support that Nb addition promotes NbC precipitation, which refines austenite grains and impedes κ-carbide coarsening without significantly altering the ferrite-austenite phase balance.

This figure presents a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) bright-field micrograph of the 0.02Nb steel (Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C-0.02Nb), showing a dark-contrast precipitate particle embedded within the γ-austenite matrix, with a scale bar of 500 nm. The region labeled "Spot 1" indicates the analyzed area. The upper-right inset displays a selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern captured from the precipitate and surrounding matrix, revealing superimposed diffraction spots from both the austenite phase (yellow-indexed reflections including 011 and 0-11) and the NbC phase (red-indexed reflections including 002, 020, and 0-22). The pattern establishes an orientation relationship of [100]γ // [100]NbC between the austenite matrix and the NbC precipitate. This direct TEM and SAED evidence confirms the presence of nano-scale NbC precipitates in the 0.02Nb low-density steel and elucidates their crystallographic orientation relationship with the parent austenite phase, supporting the conclusion that Nb microalloying promotes NbC precipitation, which contributes to microstructural refinement and strengthening.
